APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This is squarely within a top NLP/ML venue: end-to-end neural relation extraction is a core NLP task and the paper reports state-of-the-art gains (±1.9% on ACE05, 6.8% on CONLL04) over strong baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014). The formulation (table-filling with globally normalized training, beam search, and LSTM-based encoders) and evaluation (ACE05/CONLL04, micro F1, comparisons to prior work, hyperparameter reporting) match community standards. Coverage of related work is thorough and the addressed audience (NLP/ML researchers working on structured prediction and relation extraction) is appropriate, despite practical limits noted (beam size and non-overlapping entity assumption). | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—end-to-end neural relation extraction—and introduces globally normalized training and parser-augmented word representations, which are of clear interest to a top-tier NLP/ML audience. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04), reports state-of-the-art gains versus established baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014), and uses expected methods (LSTM encoders, pretrained embeddings, character-CNN, beam search) and metrics (micro F1). The problem formulation and evaluation align with community standards, and the paper is positioned appropriately despite noted practical limitations (beam-size decoding speed and non-overlapping-entity assumption).

The formulation (table-filling with globally normalized training, beam search, and LSTM-based encoders) and evaluation (ACE05/CONLL04, micro F1, comparisons to prior work, hyperparameter reporting) match community standards. Coverage of related work is thorough and the addressed audience (NLP/ML researchers working on structured prediction and relation extraction) is appropriate, despite practical limits noted (beam size and non-overlapping entity assumption).

It evaluates on standard benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04), reports state-of-the-art gains versus established baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014), and uses expected methods (LSTM encoders, pretrained embeddings, character-CNN, beam search) and metrics (micro F1). The problem formulation and evaluation align with community standards, and the paper is positioned appropriately despite noted practical limitations (beam-size decoding speed and non-overlapping-entity assumption).

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of end-to-end relation extraction and proposes a globally optimized neural approach that integrates parser hidden layers into word representations, which is a meaningful methodological contribution. It achieves state-of-the-art results—improving prior best by 1.9% on ACE05 and 6.8% on CONLL04—so the results are likely to influence how researchers combine syntactic signals and global training for this task. Practical limitations (beam decoding speed and the no-overlapping-entities assumption) constrain generality, but the clear empirical gains suggest strong impact and likely follow-on work. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of end-to-end relation extraction and proposes global optimization for a task typically handled with locally trained models. It reports state-of-the-art gains on ACE05 and CONLL04 (improvements of 1.9% and 6.8%), indicating that globally normalized training and the integration of parser hidden layers into word representations can materially improve performance and may shift practitioner choices. While the contribution is likely to spur follow-up work and be adopted within the relation-extraction community, its implications are mainly within the NLP subfield rather than broadly transformative across many areas.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper reports solid experimental evidence on two standard benchmarks (ACE05 and CONLL04) with clear comparisons to strong baselines and reports hyperparameters, and it explicitly discusses limitations (beam-size decoding speed and the no-overlapping-entities assumption). Related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses for the many architectural choices (e.g., parser-hidden-layer augmentation, segment representations), nor is code availability stated, which leaves some reproducibility and component-level attribution gaps. | B(4): The paper reports strong empirical results on two standard datasets (ACE05 and CONLL04) with state-of-the-art improvements and compares against relevant baselines, so experiments are broadly convincing. It documents some practical sensitivity (beam-size vs. decoding speed) and reports hyperparameters, and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not mention explicit ablation studies or broader sensitivity analyses of architectural components, and code availability is not stated, so reproducibility is not fully guaranteed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports solid experimental evidence on two standard benchmarks (ACE05 and CONLL04) with clear comparisons to strong baselines and reports hyperparameters, and it explicitly discusses limitations (beam-size decoding speed and the no-overlapping-entities assumption). Related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses for the many architectural choices (e.g., parser-hidden-layer augmentation, segment representations), nor is code availability stated, which leaves some reproducibility and component-level attribution gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper reports strong empirical results on two standard datasets (ACE05 and CONLL04) with state-of-the-art improvements and compares against relevant baselines, so experiments are broadly convincing. It documents some practical sensitivity (beam-size vs. decoding speed) and reports hyperparameters, and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not mention explicit ablation studies or broader sensitivity analyses of architectural components, and code availability is not stated, so reproducibility is not fully guaranteed.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a notable combination of ideas not previously unified: treating end-to-end relation extraction as a table-filling problem with three LSTM encoders and applying globally normalized training (beam search + early-update) for joint decoding, which meaningfully departs from locally trained neural pipelines. Its integration of parser hidden LSTM layers (Dozat & Manning 2016) to augment word representations is a practical, novel use of syntactic signals within an end-to-end neural model. However, many components (LSTM-Minus segment representations, pretrained/fine-tuned embeddings, POS, character-CNN) are established techniques, so the contribution is a clear, non-trivial extension rather than a wholly new paradigm. | B(3): The paper combines several existing techniques—end-to-end table-filling for relation extraction, LSTM-based segment representations (LSTM-Minus), enhanced word features (pretrained embeddings, POS, char-CNN), and global normalization with beam search/early-update—into a single globally optimized model, rather than proposing a fundamentally new paradigm. The integration of parser hidden LSTM layers from Dozat & Manning (2016) as augmented word representations and the application of global training to this joint setting are non-trivial and clearly differentiated from prior work, and they yield measurable SOTA gains on ACE05 and CONLL04. Overall this reads as a meaningful, yet incremental, advance that would inform experts but not radically surprise them.

Its integration of parser hidden LSTM layers (Dozat & Manning 2016) to augment word representations is a practical, novel use of syntactic signals within an end-to-end neural model. However, many components (LSTM-Minus segment representations, pretrained/fine-tuned embeddings, POS, character-CNN) are established techniques, so the contribution is a clear, non-trivial extension rather than a wholly new paradigm.

The integration of parser hidden LSTM layers from Dozat & Manning (2016) as augmented word representations and the application of global training to this joint setting are non-trivial and clearly differentiated from prior work, and they yield measurable SOTA gains on ACE05 and CONLL04. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments reporting state-of-the-art micro F1 improvements on ACE05 and CONLL04, and the method compares against appropriate baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014, Miwa & Sasaki 2014) with hyperparameters reported. The model design and training (three LSTM encoders, parser hidden-layer augmentation from Dozat & Manning 2016, LSTM-Minus segments, globally normalized beam-search with early-update) are technically sound and consistent with prior work. Minor concerns that do not fatally undermine the conclusions include no mention of statistical significance testing, lack of publicly available code, and the practical limits imposed by beam-size speed and the no-overlapping-entities assumption, which reduce generality but not the reported empirical gains. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: the model attains reported state-of-the-art micro F1 gains on ACE05 (+1.9%) and CONLL04 (+6.8%) against strong baselines (e.g., Miwa & Bansal 2016). There are no mathematical derivations to evaluate, and the experimental methodology appears largely sound (appropriate datasets, strong baselines, hyperparameters reported, and use of global normalization with beam search), but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or detailed ablations. The paper also makes an explicit modeling assumption (no overlapping entities with BILOU) and a practical decoding limitation (beam size ≤5) that may restrict generality; nevertheless, the conclusions about improved performance are reasonably justified by the presented results.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments reporting state-of-the-art micro F1 improvements on ACE05 and CONLL04, and the method compares against appropriate baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014, Miwa & Sasaki 2014) with hyperparameters reported. The model design and training (three LSTM encoders, parser hidden-layer augmentation from Dozat & Manning 2016, LSTM-Minus segments, globally normalized beam-search with early-update) are technically sound and consistent with prior work. Minor concerns that do not fatally undermine the conclusions include no mention of statistical significance testing, lack of publicly available code, and the practical limits imposed by beam-size speed and the no-overlapping-entities assumption, which reduce generality but not the reported empirical gains.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: the model attains reported state-of-the-art micro F1 gains on ACE05 (+1.9%) and CONLL04 (+6.8%) against strong baselines (e.g., Miwa & Bansal 2016). There are no mathematical derivations to evaluate, and the experimental methodology appears largely sound (appropriate datasets, strong baselines, hyperparameters reported, and use of global normalization with beam search), but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or detailed ablations. The paper also makes an explicit modeling assumption (no overlapping entities with BILOU) and a practical decoding limitation (beam size ≤5) that may restrict generality; nevertheless, the conclusions about improved performance are reasonably justified by the presented results.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines (e.g., Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014, Miwa & Sasaki 2014) on standard datasets (ACE05, CONLL04) using the same micro F1 metric. It reports explicit numerical gains over prior best results (+1.9% on ACE05, +6.8% on CONLL04), describes the methodological differences (global optimization, parser-derived features), and documents limitations; related work coverage is noted as thorough. Based on the provided summary, the comparisons are comprehensive and fair. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (e.g., Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014, Miwa & Sasaki 2014) and evaluates on standard datasets (ACE05, CONLL04) using micro F1. The comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and report concrete gains (1.9% on ACE05, 6.8% on CONLL04). Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper clearly contrasts its contributions (parser-hidden features, LSTM-Minus segments, global normalization) with prior methods.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from methodology (three LSTM encoders, augmented word/segment representations, global training) to experiments (datasets, baselines, micro F1 results) and conclusion. The main contributions are stated early (global optimization, integration of parser hidden layers, end-to-end table-filling framing) and hyperparameters and limitations (beam-size speed, BILOU assumption) are explicitly reported. Notation and method descriptions seem consistent and well-defined from the summary, and results are presented quantitatively (specific F1 gains on ACE05 and CONLL04), while writing quality is noted as good and related work coverage thorough, suggesting only minor presentation issues remain. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from method (three LSTM encoders, augmented word representations, segment representations, global normalization) to experiments on ACE05 and CONLL04, and its main contributions (globally optimized end-to-end model; syntactic feature integration) are stated early. Presentation is strong: related work is thorough, hyperparameters and evaluation metrics (micro F1) are reported, and limitations (beam-size decoding speed, BILOU non-overlap assumption) are explicitly discussed, which suggests figures/tables/examples are effective. Notation cannot be fully judged from this summary alone, but the overall writing quality is reported as good, so I assign 4 (well-written with minor presentation issues).

main_claims: A globally optimized neural model for end-to-end relation extraction that improves over locally trained neural models.
Integration of syntactic features by augmenting word representations with hidden LSTM layers from a state-of-the-art dependency parser (Dozat & Manning 2016).
Use of segment representations (LSTM-Minus) and enhanced word representations (pretrained/fine-tuned embeddings, POS, character-CNN) for entity detection and relation classification.
The proposed model achieves state-of-the-art results on ACE05 and CONLL04, improving previous best results by 1.9% (ACE05) and 6.8% (CONLL04).
_summary.methodology: Treat relation extraction as an end-to-end table-filling problem using three LSTM encoders (left-to-right word, right-to-left word, and entity-label LSTMs), augmented word representations (pretrained embeddings, POS, character-CNN, and parser hidden layers), segment representations via LSTM-Minus, and globally normalized training with beam search and early-update.
limitations: Decoding speed becomes intolerably slow when beam size increases beyond 5 (practical beam size chosen = 5).
Assumes no overlapping entities in a sentence (BILOU labeling scheme).
